title: Build something you'll actually use

spec.title: Build something you'll actually use
spec.notes_for_generator: Create a reusable prompt template for a recurring task. First automation instinct — the shift from one-off prompts to systems. The output should be something the learner saves and reuses. Hinted: mention the idea of a prompt template (a prompt with blanks) without providing one.

content:
SUBJECT: AI Gym Day 10: Stop Rewriting the Same Prompt Twice

You've probably typed a very similar prompt three times this week. Different details, same structure. That's time you'll never get back — and it's also the gap between using an LLM and *building a repeatable workflow with* an LLM.

TODAY'S WORKOUT
───────────────
⚙️ AUTOMATION | 🔵 STEADY STATE | ⏱ 15–20 min

THE BRIEF:
Pick one task you use a chat-based LLM for repeatedly — weekly updates, email replies, meeting notes, content outlines, whatever comes up more than once in your working life. Today you're not going to write a one-off prompt. You're going to build a reusable prompt template: a prompt with deliberate blanks that you can fill in each time and get a consistent, useful result. Think of it as a form you designed yourself.

YOUR REPS:
1. Tell the AI what recurring task you have in mind. Describe who it's for, what it typically needs to contain, and what "good" looks like for that output. Ask it to help you build a prompt template — one with clearly marked placeholders you'd swap out each time you use it. You decide what the blanks are. Don't let the AI decide everything.

2. Read the template back critically. Try filling it in mentally with a real example from this week. Does the template actually capture the variables that change? Does it over-specify things that should stay flexible, or under-specify things that always stay the same? Ask the AI to revise it based on what you find.

3. Test the template for real. Copy it, fill in the blanks with actual content, and run it as a live prompt. Compare the output to what you'd normally get from a rough, off-the-cuff prompt for the same task. Note what's better, what still needs tuning, and make one final adjustment.

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
Your output is good if:
• The template has clearly marked placeholders (e.g. [topic], [audience], [tone]) that map to things that genuinely change each time you use it
• The fixed parts of the template contain enough context that you don't have to re-explain your situation every time you use it
• A colleague unfamiliar with your workflow could pick up the template, fill in the blanks, and get a usable output without asking you questions

💡 COACHING NOTE:
A prompt template is just a system with a human in the loop. The principle here is separating what *stays constant* from what *changes* — and encoding the constant part so you don't have to carry it in your head each time. This is the same move that makes any good process repeatable: checklists, briefing docs, standard email structures. The transfer is wide: once you start noticing the recurring shape of a task, you can build a template for almost anything. Start with the task you do most often. That's where the time compounds.

──────────
Day 10 is where momentum gets interesting. You now have a reusable template, not just a one-off prompt. The risk is stopping just as this starts becoming part of your working rhythm. The 90-Day AI Habit exists to protect that momentum: more reps, harder workflows, and a practical output pack you can keep using.

Use only material you are allowed to share with your chosen LLM. Anonymise anything sensitive.
Share the programme: https://harrysharman.com/projects/ai-habit/
Found this useful? You can leave a tip: https://ko-fi.com/theaihabit
review_notes: Clarified that AI Habit focuses on chat-based large language models (LLMs), while using AI as public shorthand.
Kept exercises model-agnostic and usable with any text-based LLM or approved work tool.
Reworked research/reasoning language to avoid over-trusting model confidence or hidden chain-of-thought.
Reframed AI Habit as a grounded free 14-day starter block with a paid continuation for deeper 60-90 day habit-building, rather than a validation experiment.

Positioned the paid continuation as The 90-Day AI Habit: a structured applied-LLM programme covering writing/editing, strategy/decision-making, research/assessment, management/delegation, personal productivity, and practical outputs.
Simplified the 90-day commercial route: join individually, bring a small group for a discounted group subscription, or use the workplace launch pack to get an employer to pay.
Added ethical upgrade nudges to Days 3, 7, and 10: endowed progress, career identity, and momentum protection for The 90-Day AI Habit.
Mechanical repair pass: normalised workout badge line after scheduler verification.
